findings.summary: publishing/core.py is a pure re-export shim that pulls from split submodules and then re-exports through __init__.py, creating a two-hop indirection layer with no added policy
evidence: publishing/core.py lines 24-47 are entirely noqa:F401 re-exports from citations, metadata, platforms, and models — no logic of its own except create_publication_package
publishing/__init__.py then imports from .core and re-exports the same 20+ symbols, so callers that import from infrastructure.publishing travel through __init__ -> core -> submodule for every call
The __init__ docstring still describes 'core' as the canonical module, but core itself is now hollow delegation
findings.suggestion: Flatten publishing/__init__.py to import directly from citations, metadata, platforms, models. Remove core.py or promote its one real function (create_publication_package) into the appropriate submodule. Eliminate the double-hop.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/logging_utils.py (118 importers) acts as a mega-hub aggregating formatters, helpers, and progress utilities from split modules, then re-exposing them, making it a required transit node for the entire codebase
evidence: logging_utils.py lines 24-35 import JSONFormatter, TemplateFormatter, format_duration, format_error_with_suggestions, Spinner, StreamingProgress, and six ETA/progress functions from three sibling modules, then makes them available at the logging_utils namespace
118 files import from logging_utils — any change to logging_formatters.py or logging_progress.py has blast radius across the full codebase because the hub re-exports them

ProjectLogger class (lines 59-139) wraps a stdlib Logger and delegates every method (debug, info, warning, error, critical, success, header, progress, stage, substep) without adding any policy, creating a pass-through wrapper over an already-adequate stdlib object
findings.suggestion: Stop re-exporting sibling modules through logging_utils. Let callers import directly from logging_formatters or logging_progress where needed, or consolidate the split modules back into logging_utils if the split was premature. Remove ProjectLogger if it adds no policy over stdlib Logger.

findings.summary: validate_markdown_cli.py lives under infrastructure/validation/ but contains duplicated business logic (find_markdown_files, collect_symbols) that partially reimplements infrastructure/validation/markdown_validator.py
evidence: validate_markdown_cli.py defines its own find_markdown_files (lines 51-78) using os.listdir + manual sort, despite importing find_manuscript_directory and validate_markdown from markdown_validator

The module comment says 'All business logic is in infrastructure/markdown_validator.py' but lines 81-107 implement collect_symbols locally with inline regex, which duplicates label/ref extraction that markdown_validator already performs
An import inside a function body (import re at line 97, import os at line 61) indicates the module was assembled ad-hoc rather than following the codebase's standard import layout
findings.suggestion: Move find_markdown_files and collect_symbols into markdown_validator.py (or a shared helper), then import them in the CLI script. The CLI should be a thin argument-parser only.

findings.summary: get_config_as_env_vars is a self-described 'thin wrapper' over get_config_as_dict with only a default-argument flip — it exists solely to give an alias with a different default, which is a naming/convention outlier against every other module that does not create such aliases
evidence: config_loader.py lines 264-269: get_config_as_env_vars body is a single return get_config_as_dict(repo_root, respect_existing=respect_existing) — no added logic
The docstring explicitly says 'Thin wrapper over get_config_as_dict with respect_existing=True by default', confirming the function exists only to change the default value of one parameter
No other module in infrastructure/core/ uses this alias-with-different-default pattern; all other functions have distinct responsibilities
findings.suggestion: Remove get_config_as_env_vars and update callers to pass respect_existing=True explicitly, or change the default in get_config_as_dict. Aliases for default-value differences add surface area without boundary value.

findings.summary: setup_project_logging and get_project_logger are near-duplicate convenience constructors for ProjectLogger — two public entry points with near-identical docstrings for the same object create inconsistency in how callers initialize logging
evidence: get_project_logger (line 147) returns ProjectLogger(name, level) — body is one line
setup_project_logging (line 169) calls setup_logger if log_file provided then returns get_project_logger(name, level) — adds conditional file handler but otherwise identical
118 importers must choose between these two functions; no convention document distinguishes when to use each
findings.suggestion: Merge into a single get_project_logger(name, level=None, log_file=None) that handles the optional file handler internally. Remove the second entry point.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/errors.py (InfraError dataclass with typed constants) and infrastructure/core/exceptions.py (TemplateError class hierarchy) are parallel error systems with no cross-referencing, creating two incompatible error conventions in the same layer
evidence: errors.py defines InfraError as a frozen dataclass with a .format() method for string-based error messages (line 17-47) — callers use logger.error(PIPELINE_STAGE_FAILED.format(...))
exceptions.py defines TemplateError with rich context/suggestions/recovery_commands (line 21-67) — callers raise these as exceptions
Neither module imports from or references the other; code choosing between them gets no guidance, and the two systems serve overlapping purposes (structured error descriptions with context)
findings.suggestion: Define one pattern: either use typed constants (InfraError) for logging-side messages and typed exceptions (TemplateError) for raise-side, with explicit documentation of which to use when; or consolidate InfraError constants into TemplateError subclasses that carry the same code/suggestion fields.

findings.summary: Mixed error strategies at I/O boundaries: some functions raise custom TemplateError subclasses, others return None/False/empty-dict, and others silently swallow exceptions with only a log line — no consistent contract across the infrastructure layer
evidence: config_loader.load_config (line 151-161) catches FileNotFoundError, PermissionError, yaml.YAMLError and returns None — callers must null-check every call

rendering/core.py render_all (line 81-116) catches slide/HTML render failures with except Exception as e, logs a warning, and continues — partial failures are invisible to the caller, which receives a shorter outputs list with no error signal
integrity.py verify_file_integrity (line 77-79) catches bare Exception, logs an error, and sets integrity[path]=False — error detail is lost after the log line; callers only see a False bool

exceptions.py provides chain_exceptions and raise_with_context utilities (lines 689-665) but Grep shows only 8 call sites across two files — these utilities are largely unused while inconsistent strategies proliferate
findings.suggestion: Define a layered policy: core I/O functions raise FileOperationError (never return None); callers that want optional behavior explicitly catch and convert. Use chain_exceptions at every re-raise boundary to preserve context. Partial-failure in render_all should be surfaced as a structured warning object, not invisible list truncation.

findings.summary: validate_publication_readiness catches (OSError, UnicodeDecodeError) per file and silently continues, while sibling verify_data_consistency catches bare Exception and breaks the loop — inconsistent loop-error handling even within the same module group
evidence: publishing/core.py validate_publication_readiness (lines 282-286): catches (OSError, UnicodeDecodeError) and continues — narrow, appropriate
validation/integrity.py verify_data_consistency (lines 192-195): catches bare Exception and breaks — stops processing remaining files on first error, silently inflating integrity=False for all
validation/integrity.py verify_cross_references (lines 125-128): catches bare Exception, sets all integrity keys to False, and breaks — same pattern, same silencing
findings.suggestion: Standardize loop-error handling: catch specific exceptions (OSError, json.JSONDecodeError) per iteration; record per-file status; never use bare Exception + break to halt loop silently. Return a structured result per file so callers can distinguish 'unreadable' from 'inconsistent'.

findings.summary: rendering/pdf_renderer.py has 12 bare 'except Exception as e' clauses — the highest density in the codebase — most of which log and swallow, hiding LaTeX compilation failures from the pipeline orchestrator
evidence: Grep count shows 12 'except Exception as e' occurrences in pdf_renderer.py alone, compared to 1-4 in all other modules
Multiple clauses follow the pattern: except Exception as e: logger.warning(f'...{e}') followed by return '' or pass — the caller receives an empty path or no signal
The outer render_all in rendering/core.py also swallows slide/HTML failures (lines 81-116), compounding the loss — a LaTeX compilation error can disappear through two layers of swallowing
findings.suggestion: Replace swallow patterns in pdf_renderer with targeted raises of RenderingError or CompilationError. Let the orchestrator decide whether to continue or abort. Reserve swallow+log only for genuinely optional side steps (e.g., bibliography post-processing).

findings.summary: ProjectLogger wraps stdlib logging.Logger with pass-through methods for every log level and delegates all context managers to module-level functions — adds no policy, auth, metrics, or retry, making it a zero-value wrapper
evidence: ProjectLogger.debug (line 85-87): body is self._logger.debug(message, *args, **kwargs) — pure delegation
ProjectLogger.info/warning/error/critical follow the identical pattern (lines 89-103) with no filtering, enrichment, or routing
ProjectLogger.operation context manager (lines 125-129): body is 'with log_operation(operation, self._logger, level): yield' — delegates to a module-level function with no added state
get_project_logger (line 166) returns ProjectLogger(name, level) — the factory adds nothing over direct instantiation
findings.suggestion: Remove ProjectLogger and get_project_logger. The 118 importers that call get_logger(__name__) already have a proper stdlib Logger. If structured context (project name, stage) needs to be injected automatically, use logging.LoggerAdapter instead of a wrapper class.

findings.summary: get_config_as_env_vars is a forwarding function that only changes a default argument value — it meets the exact definition of a 'function that only forwards args/kwargs to another function without policy or translation'
evidence: Lines 264-269: entire function body is 'return get_config_as_dict(repo_root, respect_existing=respect_existing)'
The docstring describes it as a 'Thin wrapper' — the codebase itself labels this as a wrapper without added logic
find_config_file also has a private _load_project_config wrapper (lines 298-305) that does nothing but construct a Path and call load_config — two-level forwarding for a three-line operation
findings.suggestion: Delete get_config_as_env_vars. Update callers to call get_config_as_dict directly with the appropriate respect_existing value. Inline _load_project_config at its two call sites (get_translation_languages, get_review_types) to eliminate the unnecessary private wrapper.

findings.summary: validate_publication_readiness performs naive keyword-in-string-content checks (has_abstract = 'abstract' in all_content.lower()) as a proxy for document structure — the abstraction claims to validate readiness but the implementation is too shallow to be meaningful
evidence: Lines 288-292: has_abstract, has_introduction, has_methodology, has_results, has_conclusion are all boolean 'word in lowercased string' checks on concatenated file content
A file containing the word 'abstract' anywhere — in a comment, a variable name, a bibliography entry — would satisfy has_abstract=True
The function returns completeness_score as an integer built from hardcoded per-section weights (15, 15, 20, 20, 15) with no connection to actual section structure or word count
findings.suggestion: Either implement real structural validation (parse heading hierarchy, require minimum word counts per section) or remove the function and replace with a structural markdown parser. If lightweight checking is acceptable, document the limitation explicitly in the docstring and return a 'heuristic_only' flag in the result dict.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/ contains both errors.py (InfraError dataclass constants) and exceptions.py (TemplateError class hierarchy) — two parallel abstractions for the same concern with no unifying interface, forcing callers to pick arbitrarily between them
evidence: errors.py InfraError has fields: code, message, suggestion — used for log-formatted strings via .format()
exceptions.py TemplateError has fields: message, context, suggestions, recovery_commands — used for raised exceptions

Both describe the same domain (pipeline/build/validation errors with human-readable context and recovery hints) but through incompatible interfaces; callers who want to both raise and log a structured error must use both systems simultaneously
findings.suggestion: Unify by adding a code field and a to_log_message() method to TemplateError (or a subclass), so a single exception instance can serve both as a raised exception and as the source of a structured log message. Deprecate InfraError constants in favor of exception class attributes.
